sources.name: Wikipedia — Vectorman
sources.notes: Mechanics overview, morph system, shooting system.

notes: Vectorman is a late-era Genesis action platformer (1995) developed by BlueSky Software.
Vectorman is an orbot (orb robot) — a garbage-collecting android on Earth after humans have left.
DEFINING MECHANIC: Vectorman can MORPH into various vehicle/weapon forms by collecting powerups.
360-degree directional shooting — Vectorman fires in the direction the player aims, not locked to forward.
Physics: smooth analog-feel movement and physics by late-Genesis standards.
Walk and jump values not precisely documented from ROM — estimated from gameplay.

walk_speed.notes: Late-era Genesis platformer feel. Smooth movement. VERIFY.

run_speed.notes: Vectorman can run at higher speed. VERIFY.

jump.notes: Standard variable height jump with full air control.

shooting_system._note: The core combat system. 360-degree directional aiming.
shooting_system.description: Vectorman fires energy blasts in the direction the player aims using the d-pad.
shooting_system.fire_rate: Rapid — can hold button for sustained fire
notes: Diagonal aiming allows hitting enemies at angles — more flexible than forward-only games.
The 360-degree aiming is the primary distinguishing feature of combat vs same-era platformers.
Players can shoot while moving in a different direction — a coordination challenge.

morph_system._note: THE DEFINING FEATURE. Vectorman can collect powerups to temporarily transform into different forms.
morph_system.description: Collect morph powerups scattered in levels. Each powerup transforms Vectorman into a specific form with unique physics and attacks.
morph_system.duration: Time-limited. Morph expires after a set duration and Vectorman returns to normal.

ball.description: Vectorman becomes a sphere. Can roll and bounce.

drill.description: Drill form for boring through enemies and terrain.

barge.description: Large vehicle form. Slower but powerful.

other_forms.notes: Multiple additional forms — specific list not fully documented from primary sources. VERIFY complete form list.

notes: Primarily horizontal scrolling with some vertical sections.
Late-era Genesis visual polish — many transparency effects, parallax backgrounds.
Vectorman was a technical showcase for Genesis capabilities in 1995.
Enemy variety is high — many different enemy types requiring different combat approaches.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: Vectorman's 360-degree aiming moves the game toward run-and-gun shooter territory even though it plays as a platformer. Players must simultaneously manage platform navigation and free-aim combat — a higher cognitive load than forward-only shooting. The reward: enemies in any direction are immediately threatening, raising tension.

The morph system creates 'identity disruption' moments — Vectorman briefly stops being himself and becomes something else entirely. The temporary nature creates urgency (use the form efficiently before it expires) and anticipation (when will the next powerup appear?).

Vectorman as a garbage-collecting android on post-human Earth is a quiet ecological premise for a 1995 action game. The storytelling context doesn't affect mechanics at all, but it frames the visual design: ruined infrastructure, automated systems gone wrong, an empty world.

Vectorman was Sega's response to Donkey Kong Country's pre-rendered 3D sprite aesthetic — BlueSky Software used similar techniques. The orbot's spherical composition from separate orb shapes was designed specifically to showcase smooth, rotational animation. Aesthetic and mechanics were designed together.

The 1995 release date (late in SNES/Genesis lifecycle) means Vectorman benefited from years of optimization experience. The physics are smoother, the visual effects more complex, and the design more polished than many earlier Genesis titles — a late-cycle showcase.
